era_context: Egypt's Old Kingdom, Dynasty IV (c. 2613–2494 BCE). The reign of Khufu or his immediate successors; the age of the great pyramid complexes at Giza. Contemporaneously, the Akkad Empire was consolidating power in Mesopotamia under Sargon of Akkad (r. c. 2334–2279 BCE), though this is slightly later. In the Indus Valley, early urban centres were developing. This period represents the apex of Egyptian monumental architecture and centralised pharaonic power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesopotamia (limited specific events for this year)
Indus Valley (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Reign of Khufu or successor pharaoh; ongoing construction or maintenance of Giza pyramid complex
events.description: During the reign of Khufu (Dynasty IV, c. 2589–2566 BCE) or his immediate successors, the Giza pyramid complex was either under active construction, recently completed, or undergoing maintenance and ritual use. The Great Pyramid of Khufu is the largest stone structure built in antiquity. The year -2594 falls within or shortly after Khufu's reign; the exact chronology of Dynasty IV remains debated among Egyptologists, with some proposing dates up to 100 years earlier or later. The pyramid served as a royal tomb, administrative centre, and symbol of pharaonic divine power.

sources.name: Abydos King List (temple of Seti I, c. 1290 BCE)
Turin King List (Ramesside papyrus, c. 1200 BCE)
Archaeological evidence from Giza pyramid complex (excavations 19th–21st centuries)
Radiocarbon dating of organic materials from pyramid construction (modern analysis)

events.certainty_note: Khufu's reign is traditionally dated c. 2589–2566 BCE, placing -2594 near or shortly after his death. However, Egyptian chronology for the Old Kingdom is contested; some scholars place Dynasty IV 50–100 years earlier. Radiocarbon evidence suggests dates within a range of ±50 years. The year -2594 is assigned by convention to this era, but the specific events of this particular year are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of Khufu's reign (c. 2589–2566 BCE) is disputed. Some scholars, using radiocarbon calibration and astronomical calculations, argue for dates as early as 2700 BCE or as late as 2500 BCE. The 'low chronology' and 'high chronology' schools differ by up to a century. No contemporary written records document specific events in -2594; all dating is inferential. The significance of the Giza pyramids as symbols of pharaonic power is undisputed, but their exact construction timeline and the specific activities in any given year remain unknown.
historiographic_note: Egyptian chronology for the Old Kingdom relies heavily on later king lists (Abydos, Turin, Manetho), which are known to contain errors, omissions, and propagandistic elements. Contemporary written records from the Old Kingdom are sparse and mostly administrative or religious in nature. Radiocarbon dating of organic materials from pyramid construction provides some constraint but carries calibration uncertainties. Archaeological stratigraphy at Giza is complex and has been disturbed by millennia of activity. The record is heavily biased toward royal monuments and elite activities; the lives of workers, peasants, and non-Egyptian peoples are largely invisible. No specific events for -2594 are attested.

graph_edges.note: Khufu's reign spans this period; pyramid complex construction or use continues
